bc478c33-936d-427f-97f7-1890d50a9938.titulo: PPE for Stage Rigging and Operation
bc478c33-936d-427f-97f7-1890d50a9938.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Working on the setup, operation and teardown of stages exposes the technician to falls, impacts, noise, electricity and chemical products. This lesson introduces the Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) that makes this work safe. Knowing and using the right PPE is the first line of defense against accidents at events.

PPE is any device for individual use intended to protect the worker against risks that may threaten their health and safety. At events, PPE must be chosen according to the specific risks of each task (working at height, electricity, noise, dust) and must follow the applicable safety standards. Beyond choosing correctly, it is essential to use, maintain and service the equipment so that it performs its function.

  • Safety Helmet: Protects the head against impacts from objects that may fall or strike. Indispensable in rigging areas with work at height above people.
  • Safety Harness with Lanyard: Indispensable for work at height. The full-body harness keeps the worker attached to a secure structure and the lanyard arrests the fall, limiting the impact.
  • Protective Gloves: Provide protection for the hands against cuts, abrasions and sharp objects while handling structures, cables and tools.
  • Safety Boots: With a non-slip sole and a protective toe cap (usually composite or steel), they protect the feet against impacts and falling heavy objects.
  • Safety Glasses: Protect the eyes against particles, dust, debris and flying objects that may cause eye injuries during cutting, sanding or assembly.
  • Ear Protectors: Reduce exposure to excessive noise from sound equipment and tools, helping to prevent noise-induced hearing loss. They can be plug (insert) or earmuff type.
  • Respiratory Mask: In work involving dust, chemical products or toxic materials, it protects the airways. The type of filter must be suited to the contaminant.
  • Protective Clothing: Resistant, suitable garments protect the body against chemical splashes, sparks and other hazards during assembly.
  • Sunscreen and Hat: At outdoor events, they protect against excessive sun exposure during long setup shifts.
  • Electrical PPE: In electrical activities, such as lighting setup, insulating gloves and other PPE specific to electricity are used, according to the voltage level involved.
  • Welding Mask: Protects the face and eyes against radiation and sparks in welding work during the assembly of metal structures.
  • Reflective Clothing: In low-visibility settings, such as dark environments or night work, it increases the worker's visibility and reduces the risk of being run over or hit.

In practice: During the setup of an outdoor festival, the rigging crew raises a lighting truss to a height of 8 meters. The riggers who climb up wear a helmet, toe-capped boots, gloves and a full-body harness with a double lanyard, always connected to an anchor point. On the ground, the person operating the saw to cut the stage lumber wears safety glasses and ear protectors, and at night everyone wears a reflective vest because forklifts move around in the dark. Each task has its own PPE set, defined by the risk of that function.

Summary

  • PPE is the first line of defense against falls, impacts, noise, electricity and dust
  • The choice of PPE depends on the specific risk of each task
  • Using, inspecting and maintaining the equipment is as important as having it

dcb4c04c-20ea-4318-9113-e1abad8a3f90.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Occupational safety standards organize how to set up, operate and tear down events safely. This lesson introduces the main NRs and technical standards that apply to stages and structures. Knowing them helps the technician understand why each procedure exists and avoid accidents and penalties.

The Regulatory Standards (NRs) are issued by the Ministry of Labor and define mandatory safety and health requirements for different activities. At events, several NRs overlap: machinery, height, electricity and construction. There are also ABNT technical standards that guide the design and safe use of structures. Keep in mind that requirements may vary by region and local legislation, so it is important to consult professionals specialized in safety.

  • NR 12 - Safety in Work with Machinery and Equipment: Sets guidelines for the safe use of machinery and equipment, including cranes, hoists and suspension systems used in stage setup and operation.
  • NR 18 - Conditions and Work Environment in the Construction Industry: Although not exclusive to stages, it deals with safety in construction environments, a frequent situation during the assembly of stage structures.
  • NR 35 - Work at Height: Sets protective measures for activities above 2 meters from the lower level, such as the assembly of elevated structures and lighting systems. It requires risk analysis, training and anchoring systems.
  • NR 10 - Safety in Electrical Installations and Services: Deals with safety in the use of electricity, essential on stages given the intensive use of electrical equipment, lighting and sound. It requires procedures and training for anyone working on installations.
  • ABNT NBR 16489 - Suspended Work Platforms: Sets safety requirements for the use of suspended platforms, such as those used in the maintenance and assembly of stage structures.
  • ABNT NBR 14762 - Design of Steel Structures: Deals with the design of metal structures (cold-formed sections), frequently used in the construction of stages and temporary structures.
  • Venue-Specific Standards: Some venues have their own safety regulations that must be followed during shows and performances, in addition to the NRs.
  • Training and Qualification: Beyond the standards, it is essential to train the entire crew on the risks of the site, emergency procedures and the correct use of safety equipment.

In practice: Before a tour arrives at an arena, the technical producer assesses which standards apply. The assembly of the lighting truss, done at a height of 9 meters, falls under NR 35: only those with work-at-height training may climb, and the anchoring system is checked. The electric hoist that lifts the structure follows NR 12. The connection of the stage power panel complies with NR 10, carried out by a qualified electrician. The venue also has its own regulation requiring clear escape routes. Each step only proceeds once the corresponding standard is met.

Summary

  • NR 12 (machinery), NR 35 (height) and NR 10 (electricity) are the NRs most present on stages
  • ABNT standards guide the design and safe use of structures and platforms
  • Training and venue regulations complement the mandatory standards

196f5369-c01f-42ef-9008-f7cb8155f216.titulo: Technical Security: Reliability of the Systems at the Event
196f5369-c01f-42ef-9008-f7cb8155f216.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Sound, light and video rely on equipment and systems that cannot fail in the middle of the show. Technical security brings together the practices that ensure everything works reliably, protected and without interruptions. This lesson shows how to prevent failures and protect an event's production systems.

Technical security encompasses practices that ensure the integrity, confidentiality and availability of the systems, equipment, data and processes used in production. In the context of shows and performances, it ensures that operations run without unwanted interruptions, protecting both the operation and the experience of the audience and the artists. Its pillars range from equipment redundancy to protection against unauthorized access and cyberattacks.

  • Redundancy and Backup: Have backup systems for critical equipment (sound, light and video consoles) to avoid unexpected failures, and keep copies of important data such as music, videos and settings.
  • Protection against Unauthorized Access: Use passwords and strong authentication to protect systems and devices, and limit physical and logical access to authorized people only.
  • Updates and Patches: Keep software, operating systems and applications updated with the latest security fixes to mitigate known vulnerabilities.
  • Real-Time Monitoring: Use monitoring systems to track how equipment is working during the event and identify problems quickly, taking corrective measures.
  • Prior Testing: Carry out rigorous testing of equipment and systems before the event to ensure everything works and avoid problems during the performance.
  • Network Isolation: Keep the production, control and internet networks separate to avoid interference and security risks.
  • Cybersecurity: Protect systems against attacks such as malware and ransomware using firewalls and antivirus software.
  • Electrical Protection: Use overload protection devices, such as circuit breakers and stabilizers, to prevent damage to equipment.
  • Crew Training: Train crew members in security practices and emergency procedures to minimize accidental errors that compromise the operation.
  • Emergency Planning: Develop a contingency plan with actions for technical failures, power outages or other emergencies.
  • Protection against Electromagnetic Interference: Position audio, video and lighting cables in a way that minimizes electromagnetic interference that causes unwanted noise.
  • Regular Maintenance: Perform preventive maintenance on equipment to keep it in good working condition.
  • Coordination between Technical Teams: Ensure clear communication between sound, light, video and other departments to avoid configuration and operation conflicts.

In practice: At a large-scale show, the sound engineer brings a backup console already configured and with a backup of the show's memory. The lighting control network is kept isolated from the staff's internet network, preventing a Wi-Fi failure from taking down the lighting. Before the doors open, the entire signal chain is tested in a soundcheck. When one amplifier's power supply fails in the middle of the performance, the monitoring system flags the problem right away and the crew activates the redundant equipment without the audience noticing. This set of precautions is technical security in action.

Summary

  • Technical security ensures the integrity, confidentiality and availability of the event's systems
  • Redundancy, network isolation and prior testing prevent interruptions in the show
  • Electrical and cyber protection and emergency plans keep the operation reliable

37765f96-9ead-484d-9152-eec637d3bc22.titulo: What electricity is: flow and current
37765f96-9ead-484d-9152-eec637d3bc22.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Everything that switches on at an event — sound, light, LED, video — depends on electricity. Before touching any cable or outlet, the technician needs to understand what is actually 'running' inside that wire. This lesson is the foundation for everything else.

Electricity is the movement of electric charges within a conductor (usually electrons in a metal wire). When that movement is orderly, we say there is an electric current, and it is the current that carries energy from one point of the circuit to another. In this lesson you'll understand the concept of electric flow and how it is measured in Amperes.

  • Electric flow: It is the measure of the amount of electric charge that crosses a given area over a period of time. It is analogous to the flow of water in a pipe or the flow of heat: it describes the rate of movement of charges through a conductor, such as an electric wire.
  • Electric current: It is the orderly flow of electric charges (usually electrons) through a conductor. It is the current that actually carries electrical energy from one point of the circuit to another and powers the equipment.
  • Unit: Ampere (A): Current is measured in amperes (A). One ampere equals one coulomb of charge passing through a point of the conductor each second (1 A = 1 C/s). The higher the Ampere, the greater the amount of charge flowing per second.
  • Direct current (DC) vs. alternating current (AC): In direct current the flow always goes in the same direction (e.g., a battery). In alternating current the direction reverses many times per second — it is the type that reaches outlets and the power grid that feeds events.
  • Why it matters at events: Equipment is specified in power (W), but the wiring, the circuit breakers and the generator work in current (A). Knowing how to read the current is what prevents an undersized cable, overheating and a breaker tripping in the middle of the show.

In practice: A LED fixture states that it 'draws' 2.5 A. If you connect eight identical fixtures on the same circuit, the total current is 8 x 2.5 = 20 A. A circuit protected by a 16 A breaker would not handle this load and would trip — that is why the technician spreads the fixtures across more than one circuit.

Summary

  • Electric current is the orderly flow of charges (electrons) in a conductor and is what carries energy.
  • The unit of current is the Ampere (A): 1 A = 1 coulomb per second.
  • Events use alternating current (AC) in the grid and outlets; a battery provides direct current (DC).

7ec36224-e579-4743-b4e0-c1aec71424ed.titulo: Voltage (Volt): the force that pushes the current
7ec36224-e579-4743-b4e0-c1aec71424ed.conteudo_rico:
To begin: If current is the 'water flowing', voltage is the 'pressure' that makes that water move. Knowing the voltage available at the site (110 V, 220 V, three-phase) is the first piece of data the technician gathers before planning a stage's power.

Electric voltage, also called potential difference, is the quantity that measures the electrical energy per unit of charge in a circuit. It is the driving force that pushes the electrons through the conductor, allowing the flow of current. Without a voltage difference between two points, there is no current.

  • Voltage (potential difference): It measures the amount of electrical energy per unit of charge between two points of the circuit. It is the force that 'pushes' the electrons: they flow from the point of higher potential to the point of lower potential, creating the current.
  • Unit: Volt (V): Voltage is measured in volts (V) and represented by the letter 'V'. The higher the voltage between two points, the greater the force available to move the charges.
  • Where voltage comes from: It is generated by energy sources such as batteries, generators and power supplies. It can be increased or decreased by transformers, which raise or lower the voltage of a circuit.
  • Standard voltages (mind the region): The available voltage varies by location: 110 V, 127 V, 220 V, 230 V, etc. The technician must confirm which voltage exists in the stage area before connecting any equipment, because plugging a device into the wrong voltage can damage it.
  • Relationship with current and resistance: By Ohm's Law, V = I · R: voltage equals current multiplied by resistance. This relationship connects voltage, current and resistance — the subject of the next lesson.

In practice: On arriving at an event, the technician measures the outlet with a multimeter and confirms 220 V. An imported piece of equipment marked only '120 V' cannot be plugged directly into that outlet — a transformer would be needed to reduce the voltage from 220 V to 120 V, avoiding burning out the device.

Summary

  • Voltage is the potential difference: the force that pushes the electrons through the circuit, measured in Volt (V).
  • Electrons flow from higher to lower potential; without a voltage difference there is no current.
  • The grid voltage varies by region (110/127/220/230 V) and must always be confirmed before energizing.

f367b1ff-b28d-4aa9-a26c-c388fd5d5d53.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Why does a thin cable heat up and a thick one doesn't? Why does part of the energy 'turn into heat'? The answer lies in resistance. And Ohm's Law is the formula that ties together voltage, current and resistance — the most important calculation in basic electricity.

Electric resistance is the property of materials to oppose the passage of current. It limits how much current can flow and turns part of the electrical energy into heat. Understanding resistance helps the technician choose the right cables and avoid dangerous overheating in operation.

  • Electric resistance: It is the property of the material that opposes the flow of current. It limits the amount of current that passes through and converts part of the electrical energy into heat. It is represented by the letter 'R'.
  • Unit: Ohm (Ω): Resistance is measured in ohms (Ω), in honor of the German physicist Georg Simon Ohm, who formulated the law that relates voltage, current and resistance.
  • Factors that influence resistance: They depend on the material of the conductor, the physical dimensions (length and cross-sectional area), the temperature and other characteristics. Longer and thinner cables have more resistance; thicker cables have less.
  • Ohm's Law: V = I · R: The applied voltage equals current multiplied by resistance. Rearranging: I = V / R and R = V / I. The higher the resistance, the lower the current for the same voltage — and vice versa.
  • Resistance turns into heat: Because resistance turns energy into heat, an undersized cable (high resistance for the current that passes through it) heats up. That is why the cable gauge is chosen according to the current it will carry.
  • Resistors: Components made to introduce a defined resistance into the circuit, controlling the current and voltage in different parts of the system.

In practice: A 220 V piece of equipment has a resistance of 44 Ω. By Ohm's Law, the current is I = V / R = 220 / 44 = 5 A. With this value the technician already knows they need a circuit and a cable that support at least 5 A, with a safety margin above that.

Summary

  • Resistance (R) is the opposition to the flow of current, measured in Ohm (Ω), and turns energy into heat.
  • Ohm's Law relates the three quantities: V = I · R (therefore I = V/R and R = V/I).
  • More resistance means less current for the same voltage; an undersized cable heats up.

289c3d80-ceb3-418a-9ebc-342d3b2f83b8.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Every equipment spec sheet lists power in Watts. It is this number that the technician adds up to find the event's total consumption and to size the generator, cables and circuit breakers. Getting this math wrong risks overload and stopping the show.

Electrical power is the rate at which electrical energy is transferred, consumed or dissipated in a circuit — that is, how much energy the equipment uses per unit of time. It is the key quantity for understanding consumption and calculating costs and supply capacity.

  • Electrical power: It is the rate at which electrical energy is transferred or converted in a circuit — the amount of energy consumed, produced or dissipated per unit of time.
  • Unit: Watt (W): Power is measured in watts (W), in honor of James Watt. 1,000 W equal 1 kilowatt (kW). It is common to add up the power of equipment in kW to size the event's power.
  • Main formula: P = V · I: Power is the product of voltage and current. Therefore, it is directly proportional to both the voltage and the current of the circuit.
  • Other forms (with Ohm's Law): Power can also be written as P = I² · R (in terms of current and resistance) or P = V² / R (in terms of voltage and resistance).
  • Calculating current from power: Rearranging P = V · I, we get I = P / V. It is the calculation the technician uses to find out how many amperes a piece of equipment draws, knowing its power and the grid voltage.
  • Energy vs. power: the kWh: Energy consumption over time is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh): the energy used in one hour at a constant rate of 1 kW. It is the unit that appears on the electricity bill.

In practice: A sound system adds up to 4,400 W of power and the grid voltage is 220 V. The current is I = P / V = 4,400 / 220 = 20 A. Knowing this, the technician plans cables and a circuit breaker to support 20 A with headroom, distributing the load if necessary.

Summary

  • Power is the energy used per unit of time, measured in Watt (W); 1,000 W = 1 kW.
  • The central formula is P = V · I, from which we get I = P / V to find the current.
  • Energy consumed over time is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh) — the unit on the electricity bill.

ef8daa53-1ba9-4f38-8676-2c2688d861bd.titulo: Power sources: utility, auxiliary and backup
ef8daa53-1ba9-4f38-8676-2c2688d861bd.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Not every venue has enough grid power for a show. The technician needs to know the three sources — the utility grid, generators and backup systems — to ensure the event can happen anywhere and won't stop if the power fails.

An event's power can come from three complementary paths: the utility grid, auxiliary power (generators) and backup systems. Each has its role, and at large-scale events they work together to ensure continuity.

  • Utility company: generation: Electricity is produced in power plants from sources such as coal, natural gas, nuclear, hydroelectric, solar and wind, each with advantages and disadvantages in cost, availability and environmental impact.
  • Utility company: distribution and metering: After being generated, the energy passes through transmission lines and substations to the distribution network, which delivers it to the premises. Consumption is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh).
  • Distribution panel (Main Power): The power enters the premises through a distribution panel, where circuit breakers and fuses protect the system against overloads and short circuits, dividing the power into circuits for specific areas.
  • Auxiliary power: generators: When the venue's grid is insufficient or nonexistent, a generator is used — equipment that converts mechanical energy (a diesel engine) into electrical energy, creating an independent and mobile electrical system for sound, light, LED, video and support areas.
  • Backup: batteries and UPS: To avoid stoppages during grid failures, reserve systems are used: batteries (lithium-ion, lead-acid) and UPS (uninterruptible power supply), which take over the supply to critical equipment when the main power goes down.
  • Backup: fuel generators and hybrid systems: Diesel generators sustain the supply for extended periods in critical settings such as events. Hybrid systems combine batteries, generators and solar panels to provide redundancy and flexibility.
  • Backup best practices: Assess the needs and prioritize the critical equipment, choose the appropriate technology, install with qualified professionals, test regularly and perform maintenance (batteries charged, generators in order).

In practice: At an outdoor festival without enough grid power, the production installs two diesel generators: one feeds sound and light, the other serves as redundancy. The sound console and the most sensitive equipment also get a UPS, which holds the signal for a few seconds if there is a source switch, preventing the show from stopping due to a momentary failure.

Summary

  • An event's power can come from the utility company, from generators (auxiliary) and from backup systems.
  • The distribution panel (Main Power) receives the power and protects it with circuit breakers and fuses.
  • Backup (batteries, UPS, redundant generators) ensures continuity for the critical equipment.

92804f12-1a30-4cc0-b823-8fad45b6bc12.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Sizing the load is the task that separates an event that works from one that runs into trouble. It means adding up everything that consumes power, converting it into current and choosing the right generator, cables and circuit breakers — with a safety margin for the peaks.

Sizing the electrical side of a stage means determining how much power will be needed and how to distribute it to feed lighting, sound, video and other equipment. The process is methodical: gather the power ratings, add them up, convert to current, choose the distribution and the protection devices — always with headroom.

  • 1. Gather the requirements: List all the stage equipment (lights, speakers, amplifiers, projectors, effect machines) and note the power (W), voltage (V) and/or current (A) of each one.
  • 2. Calculate the total demand: Add up the power ratings (or currents) of all devices to obtain the event's total power demand.
  • 3. Determine the voltage: Confirm the voltage available in the stage area (110 V, 220 V, 230 V...), since it varies by region.
  • 4. Calculate the current: Use I = P / V, where I is the current (A), P the power (W) and V the voltage (V). It is the calculation that turns the total power into current to size cables and protections.
  • 5. Distribute and separate circuits: Distribute the loads evenly across several outlets/circuits to avoid overload. High-power equipment (such as lighting) usually requires dedicated circuits.
  • 6. Generator safety margin: The generator should never operate at its limit. As a best practice, work with 20% to 30% of free capacity, ensuring stability and absorbing consumption peaks.
  • 7. Protection, infrastructure and documentation: Use appropriate circuit breakers and fuses, distribution boxes and cables; plan the cable routing to avoid trips; document all connections. For complex demands, consult a qualified electrician or engineer.

In practice: A stage adds up to 13,200 W of equipment at 220 V. The current is I = P/V = 13,200/220 = 60 A. So this load does not run at the limit, the generator must have 20% to 30% headroom: 60 A would represent at most 70% to 80% of its capacity, meaning a generator sized for around 75 to 80 A (or the equivalent power) is chosen, and the lighting is kept on a dedicated circuit.

Summary

  • Sizing the load means adding up the power ratings, converting to current (I = P/V) and choosing cables, generator and protections.
  • Distribute the loads evenly and use dedicated circuits for high-power equipment.
  • The generator never operates at its limit: work with 20% to 30% of free capacity for the peaks.

3bb0d3a4-b779-4685-93ff-1b67a65bbc46.titulo: Management and safety of the electrical network
3bb0d3a4-b779-4685-93ff-1b67a65bbc46.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Setting up the power is half the job; managing it safely during the event is the other half. Main Power, phase balancing, grounding and monitoring are what keep the show stable from start to finish, with no risks to equipment and people.

The electrical network is one of the most critical systems of a production: sound, light, video, LED and effects depend on organized, safe and stable distribution. That is why management starts in the planning of the total load and continues throughout the operation, focusing on protection, balancing and monitoring.

  • Total load planning: The first step of management: gather the consumption of all equipment and organize how the power will be distributed among the stage, lighting, house mix, backstage, dressing rooms and support areas.
  • Main Power (main distribution): The main panel where the power from the grid or generator is received and distributed. It works as a control center, usually with main breakers, busbars, three-phase and single-phase outputs, current and voltage meters and protections against overload and short circuit.
  • Subpanels and racks: From the Main Power, the power goes to subpanels and distribution racks, which feed the specific equipment of each technical area.
  • Phase balancing: In three-phase systems (common at events), distribute the load evenly across the three phases. An imbalance can cause cable overheating, voltage drop, breaker tripping and instability in the equipment.
  • Protection and cables: Each circuit must have an appropriate breaker against overload and short circuit, and the cables must be sized to handle the current they will carry, avoiding overheating and fire risk.
  • Grounding, organization and labeling: Every system needs proper grounding for the safety of equipment and people. Cables must be organized and protected from people, vehicles and the weather; panels, cables and connections must be clearly labeled.
  • Monitoring during the event: The crew constantly tracks current consumption, voltage stability, cable and connector temperature and the operation of the breakers, spotting problems early and preventing failures.

In practice: At a three-phase event, the technician notices at the Main Power that phase A is at 80 A and phases B and C at 30 A each. This imbalance heats up the phase-A cable and can trip the breaker. They redistribute circuits across the phases until they are close (about 45 to 50 A each), restoring the system's balance and stability.

Summary

  • Management starts in the load planning and centralizes the power at the Main Power, with protections and meters.
  • In three-phase systems it is essential to balance the phases to avoid overheating, voltage drop and tripping.
  • Safety requires grounding, properly sized cables, labeling and continuous monitoring during the event.

2f994f98-a621-4c52-8870-9d38d0c22aaa.titulo: Electronics Fundamentals for the Events Technician
2f994f98-a621-4c52-8870-9d38d0c22aaa.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before setting up any sound, light or video, you need to understand what is running inside the cables: electricity. In this lesson you'll master the basic concepts that underpin the rest of the module. Without this foundation, any fault becomes a mystery.

Electronics studies the behavior and control of electrons in devices and systems. In the world of events, it is behind absolutely everything: sound consoles, moving lights, LED panels, intercom and even the UPS that protects the system. Understanding current, voltage and the basic components is the first step to working safely and diagnosing problems.

  • Electric current: It is the flow of electrons through a circuit. Measured in Amperes (A). The higher the current, the greater the 'volume' of energy passing through — that is why cables and circuit breakers have an Ampere limit that cannot be exceeded.
  • Voltage: It is the electric potential difference between two points of the circuit, measured in Volts (V). It is the 'force' that pushes the electrons. In Brazil, outlets are usually 127 V or 220 V — confusing the two can burn out equipment.
  • Resistance and Ohm's Law: Resistance (measured in Ohms, Ω) limits the flow of current. Ohm's Law relates the three: V = I × R (voltage = current × resistance). It is the most basic formula in electronics and helps explain why a thin cable heats up when drawing a lot of current.
  • Resistor: A component that limits the flow of current in the circuit. Unit: Ohms (Ω). It is inside practically every electronic device.
  • Diode: Allows the passage of current in only one direction. Used in rectifiers and protection circuits. The LED (light-emitting diode) is a type of diode — the basis of modern panels and fixtures.
  • Capacitor: Stores and releases electric charge. Used to filter signals and smooth voltage variations, helping to stabilize the equipment's power supply.
  • Transistor: Amplifies or controls the flow of current. It can work as an electronic switch or an amplifier. It is the basis of audio amplifiers and of all digital electronics.
  • Series vs. parallel circuit: In series, the components sit on a single path: the current is the same, but the voltage is divided. In parallel, they sit at common points: the voltage is the same, but the current is divided. Knowing the difference helps you understand power distribution and the connection of speakers.

In practice: At a show, the technician is about to connect a rack of amplifiers. They check that the outlet is 220 V (and not 127 V) before plugging in, because the amplifier is set for 220 V. Then they mentally calculate the total current that rack will draw, so as not to trip the breaker on the AC strip. This simple reasoning — the right voltage and the current within the limit — prevents burning out equipment and stopping the event.

Summary

  • Current (A) is the flow of electrons; voltage (V) is the force that pushes them; resistance (Ω) limits that flow, and Ohm's Law (V = I × R) relates the three.
  • Resistor, diode, capacitor and transistor are the basic components present in every event device.
  • In a series circuit the voltage is divided; in parallel the current is divided — this affects how power is distributed at the event.

53da8a89-2ba1-4aa0-a276-ddcc35459229.conteudo_rico:
To begin: When something doesn't work at the event, it is the test instrument that pinpoints where the problem is. And for everything to work safely, there is a whole family of support equipment: cases, racks, UPS units and generators. In this lesson you'll get to know the diagnostic tools and the infrastructure that keeps the system standing.

A good technician doesn't 'guess' — they measure. Test materials make it possible to check voltage, current and signals before an error turns into a loss. Support equipment (cases, racks, power), meanwhile, ensures safe transport, organization and continuity of operation even when the grid fails.

  • Multimeter: The instrument most used by the technician: it measures voltage (V), current (A) and resistance (Ω) in a single device. It serves to check whether an outlet has 127 V or 220 V, test cable continuity and identify blown fuses.
  • Voltmeter: Measures the voltage (potential difference) between two points. Today it is usually a function of the multimeter.
  • Ammeter and clamp meter: Measure the current flowing through the circuit. The clamp meter 'hugs' the cable and measures the current without needing to interrupt the circuit — very handy for checking the load of a rack in operation.
  • Audio generator: Generates test audio signals (tones) to check whether cables, consoles and speakers are transmitting the sound correctly.
  • Oscilloscope: Shows the waveform of an electrical signal on the screen, allowing you to visualize and analyze audio and video signals. Used in more advanced maintenance.
  • Spectrum analyzer: Shows how the energy of a signal is distributed across frequencies. In RF, it helps find free frequencies for wireless microphones and detect interference.
  • Case (flight case): Rigid cases for the safe transport of equipment, protecting against impacts and moisture during setup and teardown.
  • Rack: A standardized structure for mounting electronic equipment (amplifiers, processors) in an organized and ventilated way.
  • Laptop and tablet: The laptop controls systems, playback and light/sound programming; the tablet allows remote control of software and lighting, giving the technician mobility on stage.
  • Two-way radio and intercom (headset): The radio (walkie-talkie) connects the crews at a distance; the intercom (headset) connects production and the technical team in real time during the show.
  • Power distribution and protection: Extension cords, power line filters, stabilizers and AC strips distribute the power. Generators supply power where there is no grid; the UPS keeps critical equipment on during an outage, avoiding freezes and loss of programming.

In practice: One speaker lost its sound. Instead of swapping everything in the dark, the technician grabs the multimeter, sets it to continuity mode and tests the XLR cable: the device beeps on two pins, but stays silent on the third — the cable is broken. They replace only the cable and the sound comes back. Diagnosis in seconds, without improvising.

Summary

  • The multimeter is the essential tool: it measures voltage, current and resistance and tests cable continuity.
  • The oscilloscope and spectrum analyzer are more advanced diagnostic instruments (waveform and frequencies/RF).
  • Cases, racks, UPS and generators form the infrastructure that protects and keeps the system running.

3e231aad-110c-4935-b3ba-af1e0d2d0740.titulo: Cables: AC Power and Signal Transmission
3e231aad-110c-4935-b3ba-af1e0d2d0740.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The right cable in the right place is half the events technician's job. Each type of cable carries a type of information — power, audio, video, data or light. In this lesson you'll learn to recognize and choose the right cable for each application.

Cables fall into two large groups: AC power cables (alternating current), which carry energy, and signal cables, which carry audio, video and data. Choosing wrong can mean noise, quality loss, image failure or even electrical risk. Knowing each type is essential in day-to-day work.

  • Standard power cable (NBR 14136): An electrical power cable with plugs and outlets standardized in Brazil. It is the common power cable of the equipment.
  • Industrial, extension and distribution AC cables: More robust cables for demanding environments; extension cables carry power over greater distances; distribution cables are thicker and feed several pieces of equipment from a single source.
  • XLR cable (balanced audio): The professional standard for balanced audio, used in microphones, speakers and mixing consoles. The balanced signal (3 conductors) reduces noise in long cables — which is why it is preferred at events.
  • Jack / 1/4" cable (TRS and TS): An audio cable with a 1/4" plug. TS (mono, unbalanced) is used in guitars and instruments; TRS can carry stereo audio (headphones) or balanced audio (mono). Common in musical instruments and console connections.
  • Coaxial cable: A central conductor core with metal shielding that minimizes interference. It transmits audio, video and data signals — used, for example, in SDI video and RF/antenna signals.
  • HDMI cable: Transmits high-definition audio and video in a single cable, between TVs, players, consoles and video walls. It has a practical distance limit — for long runs, SDI (coaxial) or converters are used.
  • VGA cable: Analog video for older monitors and projectors. Phased out, but still shows up in legacy equipment.
  • Ethernet cable (network): Network and data connections in a LAN. At events, it is the basis of modern audio protocols (e.g., Dante) and networked lighting (Art-Net/sACN).
  • USB cable: Transmits data and power between computers, smartphones and peripherals; widely used for audio interfaces and controllers.
  • DMX cable: A digital cable that controls stage lighting equipment via the DMX protocol. Note: it looks like XLR but is specific to lighting data — it must not be confused with a microphone cable.
  • Optical cable (fiber optic): Transmits signals through light pulses, ideal for long distances and high-quality audio, immune to electromagnetic interference.

In practice: While setting up a video wall, the technician needs to bring the image from the switcher (video mixer) to a panel 30 meters from the stage. They know that a common HDMI cannot handle this distance without losing the signal, so they use an SDI cable (coaxial) with BNC connectors, which keeps the image stable over the long run. The right cable for the right distance.

Summary

  • Cables fall into AC power (energy) and signal (audio, video, data, light).
  • XLR is the balanced audio standard; DMX controls light; HDMI/SDI carry video; Ethernet carries data and network protocols.
  • The choice depends on the signal type, the distance and compatibility — the wrong cable causes noise, signal loss or failure.

7fc3caa0-049b-4a6f-9313-43b86a36111c.titulo: Audio, Video, Power and Lighting Connectors
7fc3caa0-049b-4a6f-9313-43b86a36111c.conteudo_rico:
To begin: If the cable is the road, the connector is the entrance. Plugging in the wrong connector — or doing it poorly — causes noise, bad contact and failures in the middle of the show. In this lesson you'll learn to recognize the right connectors for audio, video, power and light.

Each signal category has its typical connectors. A technician needs to identify them with their eyes closed, because in the rush of setup it is the connector that determines whether the signal passes clean or with a problem. Let's organize the main ones by family.

  • NBR 14136 connector (standard plug and outlet): The Brazilian standard for electrical power in homes and commercial venues. It is the common power plug of equipment in Brazil.
  • IEC connector (C13/C14, C19/C20): Power connectors found on computers, amplifiers and audio/video equipment. The C13/C14 is the famous power supply 'cord'; the C19/C20 handles higher currents.
  • PowerCON connector: A locking power connector, common in professional lighting, audio and video equipment. The lock prevents the cable from coming loose during the event — safer than the common plug.
  • XLR connector (audio): A 3-pin, locking connector for balanced audio in microphones, speakers and consoles. The lock and the 3 pins ensure a firm connection and low noise.
  • 1/4" / TRS connector (Jack): An audio plug for instruments, headphones and console connections. TS (mono) and TRS (stereo or balanced). Very common in guitars and keyboards.
  • RCA connector: Colored connectors found on home stereos and TVs, for consumer-level audio and video. Appears on simpler equipment.
  • SpeakON connector: A locking connector for connecting amplifiers to speakers (PA systems). It handles high power safely and replaces older connections using 1/4" or binding posts.
  • HDMI / DisplayPort connector: HDMI carries high-definition audio and video in a single connector; DisplayPort is similar, used for high-quality video between computers and screens.
  • VGA / DVI connector: VGA is analog video (older monitors and projectors); DVI is digital video between computers and monitors. Both phased out, but present in legacy equipment.
  • BNC connector: A bayonet-type connector used in professional video (SDI), cameras and broadcast equipment. It locks with a quarter turn, ensuring firm contact.
  • DMX connector (data XLR): A connector used to control lighting via the DMX protocol. Physically similar to the audio XLR (usually 3 or 5 pins), but dedicated to lighting data.

In practice: When connecting the PA speakers, the technician uses cables with SpeakON instead of 1/4". The SpeakON locks with a twist, handles the amplifier's high power without heating up and runs no risk of coming loose from the vibration of the bass. The result: a firm, safe connection even at the peak of the show.

Summary

  • Power: NBR 14136, IEC (C13/C14, C19/C20) and PowerCON (locking, professional).
  • Audio: XLR (balanced), 1/4"/TRS (instruments) and SpeakON (power speakers).
  • Video: HDMI, DisplayPort, VGA/DVI (legacy) and BNC (professional SDI); DMX for lighting.

c221de8e-0715-4f04-9df9-e333fbdd61f6.titulo: Wireless Transmission: RF, Infrared and Interference
c221de8e-0715-4f04-9df9-e333fbdd61f6.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Wireless microphones, intercom, remote control: more and more the signal travels through the air. But the air is a contested space, full of interference. In this lesson you'll understand how wireless transmission works and how to keep it from failing at the worst moment.

A wireless transmission carries audio, video or data from a transmitter to a receiver without cables, using electromagnetic waves. The two technologies most present at events are RF (radio frequency) and IR (infrared). And where there are many signals in the air, the great enemy appears: interference.

  • How wireless works (modulation): The original signal is combined with a higher-frequency carrier wave — a process called modulation. This allows it to be carried through the air as an electromagnetic wave and recovered at the destination (demodulation).
  • Transmission, propagation and reception: After being modulated, the signal becomes an electromagnetic wave sent by an antenna; it propagates at the speed of light, and may pass through obstacles depending on the frequency. The receiving antenna captures the wave and converts it back into an electrical signal.
  • RF (radio frequency): The band of the electromagnetic spectrum between audio and microwaves, from a few kHz to hundreds of GHz. It passes through obstacles and reaches long distances — the basis of wireless microphones, intercom, Wi-Fi, radio and TV. Its use is regulated by government agencies (in Brazil, Anatel) to avoid frequency conflicts.
  • IR (infrared): Electromagnetic radiation beyond visible light, with a longer wavelength. It generally requires a direct line of sight and has a short range. Used in remote controls, automation and sensors; also in thermal imaging (viewing heat).
  • RF vs. IR — when to use each: RF passes through walls and covers long distances, but competes for spectrum space and suffers interference. IR does not pass through obstacles and needs a direct aim, but is immune to RF interference — good for point-to-point controls.
  • RF interference (RFI): The unwanted presence of RF signals that disrupt the operation of equipment. It comes from other transmitters, mobile phones, LED lamps/dimmers, motors and wireless devices. It can cause hiss, signal dropout and even total microphone failure.
  • How to reduce interference: Electromagnetic shielding of sensitive equipment; filtering of unwanted frequencies; proper grounding; positioning (keeping away from noise sources and using directional antennas); using frequencies different from the interference sources; and automatic features such as power control and error correction.

In practice: Before an event with 8 wireless microphones, the technician uses the spectrum analyzer (or the receiver's scan function) to map which frequencies are free at that venue. They keep the receiving antenna away from LED panels (a source of RFI), coordinate the channels on different frequencies and run a walk test across the stage. This way they prevent two microphones from fighting over the same frequency and the LED from 'dirtying' the signal during the performance.

Summary

  • Wireless carries signals through the air via electromagnetic waves (modulation on sending, demodulation on receiving).
  • RF passes through obstacles and reaches far (microphones, intercom, Wi-Fi); IR needs a direct aim and has a short range (remote controls).
  • RF interference (RFI) comes from other equipment; it is mitigated with shielding, grounding, positioning and frequency choice.

a4234fea-de7d-4e22-afa7-fee330a48b3d.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Everything that goes up on stage has to stay firmly in place — and safely above people's heads. Spotlights, speakers, screens and set pieces hang from structures that leave no room for error. In this lesson you'll get to know the main rigging structures and what must never be overlooked when it comes to safety.

Rigging structure ensures the stability and, above all, the safety of the elements that are suspended and supported on stage. Since the events technician works close to these setups, they need to recognize each structure and understand that safety always comes first.

  • Grid: A truss structure (metal bars) suspended high above the stage, used to mount lighting, loudspeakers, screens and other elements at different heights and positions.
  • Fly bars (battens): Horizontal bars on the grid to which lighting, sets and curtains are attached, and which can be raised and lowered as the scene requires.
  • Lighting towers: Free-standing (floor) structures that support spotlights, moving lights and gobos, positioned around the stage to direct the light.
  • Anchor points: Points on the structure designed and tested to bear suspended loads, used to rig equipment safely. The strength of each point is verified before use.
  • Fly systems: A set of cables and pulleys that allow equipment to be suspended and moved in a controlled way, adjusting the height of sets and lighting efficiently.
  • Ground structures: Floor-based rigging: stands (tripods) for speakers, racks for audio and video, and various pedestals. An alternative when suspension isn't used.
  • Wall mounts: For lightweight equipment, such as monitors and small screens, common in control rooms and display areas.
  • Tracks and mounting bars: Tracks or bars on which equipment is mounted or slid to adjust position quickly and flexibly.
  • Safety first: Every rigging structure must be designed, installed and tested by experienced professionals, following safety standards for live events. It's essential to consider the weight of the equipment, load distribution, stability and the load capacity of the rigging. Suspended loads require secondary safety cables.

In practice: When hanging a moving light on the grid truss, the technician doesn't rely on the clamp alone. They also run a steel safety cable connecting the fixture to the structure. If the clamp fails, the safety cable holds the fixture and prevents a dangerous fall onto the audience or the crew. A suspended piece of equipment is never left without this redundancy.

Summary

  • Suspended structures (grid/truss, battens, fly systems) and ground ones (towers, racks, tripods) support the stage equipment.
  • Anchor points are tested for strength and define where loads can be suspended safely.
  • Safety is the priority: respect load capacity, use safety cables and rely on qualified professionals.

7891f0c9-ac8c-44e2-a00b-2ccb3c854da8.titulo: Lighting fundamentals: what light does at an event
7891f0c9-ac8c-44e2-a00b-2ccb3c854da8.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Lighting isn't just about brightening a room. It's about creating atmosphere, highlighting what matters and conveying emotion. In this lesson you'll understand the basic concepts behind any lighting design.

Before touching any equipment, the technician needs to master the vocabulary of light. There are only a few concepts, but they come up all the time in the day-to-day of an event.

  • What lighting is: The controlled use of light to create the desired visual effects on stages, at events, in architectural spaces and in studios. It goes beyond providing brightness: it creates atmosphere, highlights elements and conveys emotions.
  • Light source: The physical source that emits the light (incandescent lamp, LED, fluorescent, etc.). The choice of source affects the color temperature, the intensity and other characteristics of the lighting.
  • Luminous intensity: Refers to the brightness or amount of light emitted. It's measured in lumens (lm). The higher the intensity, the more lit the environment or object appears. In stage control, the intensity of each fixture is usually adjusted as a percentage (0 to 100%).
  • Direction of light: Where the light strikes the object or scene from. It's decisive in creating shadows and enhancing features. Light can come from above, from below, from the side (sidelight) or from specific angles to achieve the desired effect.
  • Beam angle: The spread of the beam emitted by the source. Narrow beams (spot) concentrate the light on a smaller area; wide beams (flood) spread the light over a larger area.
  • Diffusion and focus: Light can be directed and shaped with reflectors, lenses and accessories. Diffusion softens the light (making it more spread out and without hard edges); focus directs the light to a specific point.
  • Safety as a foundation: At live events, adequate lighting is essential for the safety of artists and crew: everyone needs to see in order to move and work without risks. Access ways, stairs and backstage areas should never be left in total darkness.

In practice: At a show in a mid-sized venue, the lighting designer calls for a 'welcome light' as the audience comes in: broad, soft, warm light covering the stage. When the show starts, they switch to narrow, focused beams on the vocalist. Same room, same fixtures, two completely different effects, just by changing direction, beam angle and diffusion.

Summary

  • Lighting is about controlling light to create atmosphere, highlight and stir emotion, not just to brighten
  • The core concepts are: source, intensity (lumens), direction, beam angle and diffusion/focus
  • Safety comes first: no one works or moves around in total darkness

25e6d5e3-1696-48fb-ba10-dd5fdf3461c1.titulo: Color temperature and the use of color in light
25e6d5e3-1696-48fb-ba10-dd5fdf3461c1.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The same scene can feel cozy or cold depending on the color of the light. In this lesson you'll understand color temperature in Kelvin and how colors communicate emotions on stage.

Color is one of the lighting designer's most powerful tools. It sets the mood of the scene before the audience even notices. Let's separate two topics: the color temperature of the source and the meaning of the colors applied.

  • Color temperature: A measure of the color appearance of the white light emitted by a source, expressed in Kelvin (K). Watch out for what seems counterintuitive: warmer (yellowish) sources have a LOWER color temperature, and cooler (bluish) sources have a HIGHER color temperature.
  • Practical Kelvin references: As a standard industry guide: warm/yellowish light sits around 2700–3200 K (intimate, cozy mood); neutral light around 4000 K; cool/bluish-white light from 5600–6500 K (modern, clinical, bright-daylight mood). Mixing sources of very different Kelvin in the same scene tends to make the light look 'dirty'.
  • Color from filters and from LED: Sources can emit different colors. Conventional fixtures use gel (a color filter) in front of the lens; LED fixtures generate color electronically by mixing the diodes (RGB / RGBW / RGBA). Combining colors creates dramatic effects and specific atmospheres.
  • Warm colors and their meanings: Red: passion, energy, love, action, attention, danger. Orange: enthusiasm, vitality, determination, warmth, friendliness. Yellow: joy, happiness, creativity, clarity, optimism, positive energy. Pink: tenderness, romance, softness, compassion.
  • Cool and neutral colors and their meanings: Blue: calm, serenity, trust, stability, depth. Green: nature, renewal, growth, health, balance. Purple: royalty, spirituality, mystery, luxury, creativity. White: purity, peace, simplicity, cleanliness. Black/absence of light: mystery, elegance, formality.
  • Color is cultural and subjective: These associations aren't universal: they vary with culture, context and personal experience. Black, for example, means mourning in some cultures and elegance in others. Use colors as a communication tool, but consider the audience and the theme of the event.

In practice: At a graduation, the entrance of the graduates calls for warm light at ~3000 K with amber and gold for a solemn, welcoming mood. When the party kicks off, the technician turns everything to saturated blue and magenta with cool backlight, and the same room becomes a nightclub. The choice of Kelvin and color completely changed the emotional reading of the moment.

Summary

  • Color temperature is measured in Kelvin: warm is low Kelvin, cool is high Kelvin
  • Color can come from gel (conventional) or from mixing LEDs (RGB/RGBW)
  • The meaning of colors communicates emotion, but it's cultural and subjective

7ff793e3-ec3c-49bb-952a-0bca4dfe2ce8.titulo: The role of the lighting technician
7ff793e3-ec3c-49bb-952a-0bca4dfe2ce8.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Behind every well-lit scene there's a technician who rigged, configured, operated and solved problems in real time. In this lesson you'll understand exactly what's expected of this role.

The lighting technician works in events, theater, TV, film and related productions. Their responsibilities vary with the scale of the event, but there's a core set of duties that always repeats.

  • Installation and setup: Rigging and configuring all the necessary lighting equipment: fixtures, cables, mounts (truss/clamps), consoles and peripherals. It's the foundation of everything and where most errors are prevented.
  • Operating the equipment: During the event, operating the fixtures according to the needs of the director, the lighting designer or the crew, following the cue sheet.
  • Programming the lights: Programming the lights according to the event's requirements, creating different looks (cues/memories) and effects on demand. In many cases this is done ahead of time during the sound/light check.
  • Maintenance and repair: Performing regular maintenance on the equipment to ensure everything works, and carrying out repairs when needed. A fixture that won't turn on at showtime is almost always a fault that could have been caught during maintenance.
  • Collaborating with the team: Working closely with directors, lighting designers, sound technicians and the rest of the production, so that the light meets the needs of the event.
  • Electrical and structural safety: Ensuring that electrical installations and equipment comply with safety standards. This includes appropriate electrical load, grounding, correct rigging of fixtures at height and safety cables.
  • Live problem-solving: At a live event, the unexpected happens. The technician needs to identify and solve it quickly to minimize the impact: a loose cable, a fixture that went dark, a jammed channel. Calm and method are worth more than haste.

In practice: Ten minutes before doors open and a front spotlight won't turn on. Instead of panicking, the technician follows the method: checks whether the fixture is addressed, tests the power cable, tests the DMX cable, and discovers that a DMX connector came loose on the truss. Reconnects, tests the cue, and the show opens on time. Problem-solving is routine, not the exception.

Summary

  • The technician installs, configures, operates, programs and maintains the lighting equipment
  • Electrical and structural safety is a central responsibility of the role
  • Solving problems live with method and speed is part of the routine

55da8aec-531e-4f44-b366-f6b0a8a85983.titulo: Lighting equipment: types of fixture and stage lights
55da8aec-531e-4f44-b366-f6b0a8a85983.conteudo_rico:
To get started: PAR, Fresnel, ellipsoidal, LED, moving head... each fixture has a beam and a purpose. In this lesson you'll learn to recognize the main pieces of equipment and know when to use each one.

Choosing the right fixture is half the job. Each type of fixture delivers a type of beam; each effect fixture serves a function. Let's go from the conventional to the automated.

  • PAR / Par Can: A rugged, versatile fixture that emits an intense, concentrated beam with no adjustable focus (the beam is set by the lamp/lens). Great for washing areas and delivering a lot of color. Today the LED PAR is very common, changing color electronically and consuming less energy.
  • Fresnel: A fixture with a Fresnel lens (ringed), which allows the focus to be adjusted from spot (tight beam) to flood (wide beam). It produces soft light with diffuse edges, good for covering wide areas with a smooth transition.
  • PC (Plano-Convex): A fixture with a plano-convex lens. It delivers a more defined beam with a slightly harder edge than the Fresnel, also with adjustable focus. Useful for highlighting areas and objects with more crispness. Technical note: the PC's lens is plano-convex; the fixture that uses lenses allowing precise shaping with shutters and gobos is the ellipsoidal, described next.
  • Ellipsoidal (profile): A fixture with a precise, defined beam, with shutters to shape the outline of the light and a holder for gobos (masks that project patterns). It's the standard tool for lighting a specific object or person with clean edges. (In the source material this precise-beam feature appears associated with the 'PC'; the profile fixture as such is the ellipsoidal.)
  • Moving Head: A motorized fixture controlled remotely: it adjusts direction (pan/tilt), color, focus and movement effects. There are spot (defined beam, with gobo), wash (wide, soft beam) and beam (very tight, powerful beam) versions. It's the 'Swiss Army knife' of the modern show.
  • Wash: A fixture that emits broad, uniform light, covering large areas with homogeneous color and intensity. Used to fill the stage or the set with color.
  • LED: Fixtures that use light-emitting diodes to generate multiple colors and effects with low energy consumption and little heat. Found in PARs, washes, bars, panels and moving heads.
  • Conventional fixtures: Traditional fixtures: PAR lamps, tungsten fixtures and the like. They're still widely used, generally with warm light and intensity control via dimmer.

In practice: At a band show, the design combines fixtures: LED PARs provide the stage's background color (a general wash), ellipsoidals cut a clean shaft on the vocalist on the floor, and beam moving heads slice through the air with haze for the choruses. Each fixture was chosen for the type of beam it delivers.

Summary

  • PAR washes and delivers color; Fresnel and PC adjust focus; the ellipsoidal shapes precisely and takes a gobo
  • The moving head is motorized (pan/tilt, color, focus) and comes in spot, wash and beam versions
  • LED delivers varied color with low consumption and little heat, in many form factors

055146a4-f5dd-4bae-8256-a95dd268b84c.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Haze cut by a beam, a strobe on the chorus, dozens of fixtures changing color at the same time. All of this depends on well-used special effects and on a control protocol: DMX.

This lesson closes the module by bringing together the effect fixtures and the system that commands all of them in sync. Understanding the basics of DMX is what separates pushing a button from operating a show.

  • Strobe: Produces intermittent flashes of light. Depending on the flash rate, it creates the effect of freezing movement or of slow-motion movement. Use it responsibly: an intense strobe can cause discomfort and there are restrictions for photosensitive people.
  • Atmosphere machines: Smoke machines, haze machines (hazers) and bubble machines. They aren't light, but they give body to the air so that the beams become visible. Without smoke/haze, a beam in the air barely shows.
  • Laser: Generates concentrated, colored beams that create complex patterns and visual effects. It requires special safety care and, often, rules regarding projection toward the audience.
  • UV light (black light): Emits ultraviolet light that makes fluorescent substances glow, creating striking visual effects on sets, costumes and special paint.
  • Other visual resources: Image projectors (displaying images, videos and patterns on surfaces), fiber optics (points of light and starry skies) and programmable intelligent lights synced with the music or the stage action.
  • What DMX is: DMX (DMX512) is the standard lighting control protocol. It allows the intensity, color and patterns of many fixtures to be adjusted precisely and in sync from a console. It replaces the idea of 'one switch per light' with a single data cable controlling everything.
  • DMX address and channels: Each fixture is given a DMX address and occupies a number of channels (for example, an LED par may use separate channels for red, green, blue and intensity). A DMX universe has 512 channels. Addressing correctly is what makes each fixture 'understand' the command aimed at it.
  • Wiring and basic care: DMX fixtures are wired in a chain (daisy chain) with the proper cable, usually running out of the console and passing from fixture to fixture. Best practices: use good-quality cable and, on long runs, a terminator on the last fixture to prevent signal noise.

In practice: Before the New Year's countdown, the technician addresses each moving head in a DMX universe, wires everything in a daisy chain from the console and places a terminator on the last fixture. At the countdown, a single cue fires: the hazer fills the air, all the beams rise together in blue and the strobe kicks in on the beat. Dozens of fixtures, one command, thanks to DMX.

Summary

  • Effects like strobe, smoke/haze, laser and UV add impact, but require safety care
  • DMX512 is the standard protocol that controls many fixtures precisely and in sync
  • Each fixture has an address and occupies channels; a DMX universe has 512 channels

341e82f6-b38e-4692-873a-c73bb39f59bf.titulo: The video profession at events: what the technician and the VJ do
341e82f6-b38e-4692-873a-c73bb39f59bf.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Working with video at events means turning ideas into moving images that inform, entertain and move the audience. Before touching any equipment, you need to understand the role of the video professional and where they work. In this lesson you'll get to know the profession, the VJ's role and why video has become a central part of any modern event.

The video professional (videomaker) creates, produces and edits images. Within the live event, one specialization stands out: the VJ (Video Jockey), who manipulates visuals in real time during the performance.

  • What the video professional is: The person who creates, produces and edits videos that inform, entertain and inspire. They combine creativity, technical knowledge and communication to deliver quality audiovisual content on online platforms, TV, film and, most importantly for us, live events.
  • What a VJ (Video Jockey) is: The professional who creates and manipulates visual content in real time during live performances: concerts, parties, cultural events and artistic performances. Just as the DJ works the sound, the VJ works the image, syncing it with the music and the atmosphere of the event.
  • Why video matters at an event: The demand for audiovisual content is growing on every front. At an event, the image amplifies the atmosphere, reinforces the message from the stage and creates a sensory experience that engages the audience. Screens and panels have become an essential part of the scenography.
  • Visual storytelling: The video professional tells stories with images. At an event, that means choosing and arranging the visual content so that the sequence of images makes sense with the music, the theme and the moment of the performance.
  • The difference between production and operation: In production (pre-event) you create and organize the content: videos, loops, animations and images. In operation (during the event) you play back, mix and adjust that content live. The junior technician usually starts out supporting the operation.

In practice: At an electronic music show, while the DJ plays, the VJ projects animation loops synced to the beat on the screens behind the stage. When the music changes tempo, they switch the visuals to match the mood — dark and slow in the calm parts, colorful and fast on the drops.

Summary

  • The video professional creates and edits images; the VJ manipulates them live at the event.
  • Live video serves to amplify the atmosphere and engage the audience.
  • Production happens before; operation happens during the event.

836dd430-7b58-4daa-906f-6c68d34f6c7c.titulo: The VJ's specific equipment: the basic video kit at an event
836dd430-7b58-4daa-906f-6c68d34f6c7c.conteudo_rico:
To get started: A good visual result depends on the right equipment, well connected and protected. In this lesson you'll get to know the basic kit that underpins any VJ operation — from the computer that is the heart of the system to the backup cables that save the performance. Knowing each piece avoids panic at showtime.

The choice of equipment varies with the style of the show, the size of the audience and the VJ's preferences. But there's a basic set that almost always appears. Let's go through it piece by piece.

  • Computer: The core of the VJ's equipment. It needs to be powerful to run the VJing software and process video in real time without freezing. A dedicated graphics card, enough RAM and fast storage (SSD) make a direct difference in performance.
  • VJing software: Specialized programs to mix and manipulate videos, images and effects live. The most cited are Resolume, VDMX and Modul8. This is where the content is organized in layers and triggered during the performance.
  • MIDI controller or VJ controller: A physical interface with buttons, sliders and pads. Each control is mapped to a software parameter — triggering a clip, changing the speed, applying an effect — allowing you to operate with your hands rather than just the mouse.
  • Projector or screens (LED vs. projection): The visuals are displayed on screens or surfaces. There are two main routes: LED screens, which are panels that emit their own light (more brightness, good for bright environments), and projection, where a projector casts the image onto a surface (cheaper over large areas, but loses sharpness with ambient light).
  • Video cables and adapters: Connect the computer to the projector or the screens. The most common are HDMI, DisplayPort and VGA. VGA is analog and old; HDMI and DisplayPort are digital and preferable. Adapters resolve incompatibilities between connectors.
  • Video capture interface: Equipment that brings an external signal — for example, from a live camera — into the VJing software. This lets the VJ incorporate live images from the stage or the crowd into the visual mix.
  • Visual content (media banks): The videos, loops, images and animations used to build the visuals. The VJ can create their own content or use existing media libraries, always organized and named to be found quickly during the show.
  • DMX lighting controller: Some VJs also command the stage lighting in sync with the visuals. The standard for lighting control at events is DMX, which connects the console/controller to the fixtures and moving heads.
  • Projection mapping tools: When projecting onto unconventional surfaces (buildings, objects, irregular sets), mapping tools are used to fit the image to the shape of the surface. This is video mapping in its basic form.
  • USB hub / power hub: MIDI controllers, capture interfaces and other devices connect via USB. A hub provides extra ports and, when powered, enough power for all the peripherals to work together.
  • Accessories and protective equipment: Spare cables, adapters and mounts ensure a stable setup. Power conditioners, voltage adapters and backup cables keep an electrical problem or a loose cable from taking down the performance.

In practice: While setting up a party, the technician connects the laptop to the LED panel via HDMI cable, plugs the MIDI controller into a powered USB hub and keeps a second, already-tested HDMI cable stored in the bag as a backup. Before the audience comes in, they run a camera signal through the capture interface to confirm the live image appears in the software.

Summary

  • The computer with VJing software (Resolume, VDMX, Modul8) is the heart of the system.
  • Display is via LED screens (which emit light) or projection; connection via HDMI, DisplayPort or VGA.
  • Hubs, power conditioners and backup cables ensure a stable, trouble-free operation.

3885e7fc-1c88-44f5-a39e-469a4b36c088.titulo: Video signal technical fundamentals: resolution, connections and switcher
3885e7fc-1c88-44f5-a39e-469a4b36c088.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Knowing how to plug in a cable is the beginning; understanding the signal that runs through it is what separates the amateur technician from the professional. In this lesson you'll master the concepts that underpin every video operation at an event: resolution, the HDMI and SDI signals, and the switcher that chooses what goes to the screen.

This is the technical enrichment content, standard in the event video industry, that complements the equipment covered earlier. Knowing these fundamentals avoids a cropped image, no signal, or the wrong resolution.

  • Resolution: The number of pixels that make up the image. The most common at events are Full HD (1920x1080) and 4K (3840x2160). The rule of thumb: the resolution coming out of the computer must match that of the screen. Sending a resolution the screen doesn't support results in a cropped, stretched or signal-less image.
  • HDMI signal: A digital connection that carries image and audio on the same cable. It's the most common standard on laptops, projectors and screens. It has a distance limit: long HDMI cables (above ~10-15 m) can lose the signal without an amplifier or extender.
  • SDI signal: A professional connection (BNC connector, which screws on) used in larger setups. Its big advantage over HDMI is distance: SDI runs dozens of meters over coaxial cable without losing quality, ideal for connecting cameras and stages far from the control area.
  • DisplayPort and VGA: DisplayPort is digital, common on computer graphics cards, and supports high resolutions. VGA is analog and old — it still shows up on old projectors, but it only carries video (no audio) and loses quality. Whenever possible, prefer digital connections.
  • Switcher: The equipment (or software) that receives several video sources — cameras, the VJ's laptop, slides — and decides which one appears on the screen at any given moment. It allows cutting from one source to another with clean transitions, being the center of the operation at events with multiple inputs.
  • The concept of source and output: A source (input) is where the video comes from (camera, computer, player). An output is where it goes (screen, projector, recording, broadcast). Every operation consists of routing sources to outputs; understanding this flow avoids plugging the cable into the wrong place.
  • Frame rate: How many images per second the video displays, measured in fps (e.g., 30 or 60 fps). Keeping the same rate across source, switcher and screen avoids stutters and a 'jumping' image. It's a setting that usually goes unnoticed until it causes trouble.
  • Basic mapping: Mapping (projection mapping) is adjusting the image to fit exactly the shape of the projected surface, even if it's irregular. In its basic form, it's aligning the corners of the projection (keystone/four-point) so the image sits straight and at the right size on the screen or the set.

In practice: At a conference, three cameras reach the control area via SDI cable (because the stage is far away) and the slides laptop arrives via HDMI. Everything goes into the switcher, and the technician sets the output to Full HD 1080p at 30 fps to match the LED screen. During the event, they cut between the cameras and the slides using the switcher, always checking that the output resolution matches the panel's.

Summary

  • Output resolution must match that of the screen; otherwise the image crops, stretches or disappears.
  • HDMI is digital and short-range; SDI is professional and covers long distances over coaxial cable.
  • The switcher receives several sources and chooses which one goes to the output.

31d41a99-6b32-42e4-9ed3-a67e53cbc5a8.titulo: The VJ in practice: mixing, syncing and improvising live
31d41a99-6b32-42e4-9ed3-a67e53cbc5a8.conteudo_rico:
To get started: With the equipment set up and the signal understood, it's time for what the audience actually sees: the live operation. In this lesson you'll walk through the VJ's day-to-day activities — from creating the content to improvising in the heat of the show — and understand that technique and reading the crowd go hand in hand.

The activities vary with the type of event, the musical style and the available technologies. But the goal is always the same: to create an engaging visual experience synced with the live music.

  • Creating visual content: Before the event, the VJ produces videos, animations, effects and images using editing, animation and design software. This material becomes the media bank that will be triggered during the performance.
  • Video mixing: Live, the VJ combines different videos and visuals in layers, creating a continuous flow of images that syncs with the music and the theme of the event. It's the visual equivalent of mixing audio tracks.
  • Real-time manipulation: During the performance, they adjust colors, transitions, effects and filters on the fly, adapting to the atmosphere and the rhythm. Nothing is fully pre-recorded: the operation responds to the moment.
  • Syncing with the music: The VJ works alongside the musical artists to match the visuals with the music. Software allows the speed and rhythm of the visual elements to be adjusted according to the soundtrack, creating a cohesive sensory experience.
  • Projection and mapping: At special events, they apply mapping techniques so the visuals adapt to irregular surfaces — buildings, three-dimensional objects, sets — generating higher-impact effects.
  • Selecting and remixing existing content: Besides their own material, the VJ selects and remixes pre-existing content (music videos, film clips, animations) to fit the visual narrative of the performance.
  • Interactivity: Some VJs incorporate interactive elements, letting the audience influence the visuals through motion sensors or mobile apps, making the experience participatory.
  • Collaborating with artists: The VJ works alongside DJs, musicians and stage artists to deliver a cohesive multimedia experience, in which sound, light and image talk to each other.
  • Technical management: Beyond creating, the VJ sets up and operates the necessary projection, lighting and control equipment — setup, testing and adjustments are part of the job.
  • Creative improvisation: The hallmark of VJing: improvising and adapting the visuals according to the audience's feedback and changes in the environment. The ability to react to the unexpected is as important as the prepared content.
  • Keeping up with trends: A good VJ stays attentive to new technologies, effects and emerging visual styles to keep performances current and engaging.

In practice: At a party, the VJ notices the dance floor has cooled down. In real time, they swap the dark loops for more colorful images and speed up the transitions to match a livelier track the DJ has just dropped. It wasn't in the plan — it's pure creative improvisation, reading the crowd's reaction.

Summary

  • The VJ creates the content beforehand and mixes, adjusts and syncs it live during the event.
  • Syncing the image with the music and collaborating with the artists is essential.
  • Creative improvisation — reacting to the crowd in real time — is the hallmark of VJing.

be197888-e601-498f-a6bc-c318107d0e00.titulo: Introduction to IT for events: software, media and streaming
be197888-e601-498f-a6bc-c318107d0e00.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Behind every image on the screen there's IT: performance software, organized media banks and, increasingly, online streaming. In this lesson you'll understand how information technology intertwines with live video and which best practices keep everything running without freezing.

IT is a fundamental tool for the VJ: it allows them to create, manipulate and project visuals in real time. Let's look at the key points of this integration between technology and performance.

  • Visual performance software: Specific programs to create and manipulate visual content in real time, combining videos, images, animations and effects in sync with the music. They're the digital foundation of the entire VJ operation.
  • Media banks: Organized collections of video clips, animation loops, images and effects. Organizing and naming this library well is what lets you find and trigger the right media quickly during the performance.
  • MIDI controllers and specialized hardware: Give more intuitive manipulation: instead of relying only on the mouse, the VJ adjusts playback speed, transitions and effects with physical buttons and sliders mapped in the software.
  • Video mapping: A technique in which projections are adjusted to fit unconventional surfaces — buildings, three-dimensional objects — creating impressive visual effects that seem to be part of the structure itself.
